facts_disclosed: Grief retreat at Lake Junaluska — April 4-6, 2025

turns.content: I came back from a retreat a few weeks ago and I am still carrying it with me. I wonder if that is normal — for something to settle in slowly, like rain soaking into dry ground. I thought I would process it there, at the lake, and come home finished. Instead I came home and the processing began.

That sounds like the retreat did exactly what it was supposed to do — not resolve everything in the moment, but open something that needed time to unfold. Some experiences are like that. They don't give you answers at the retreat center; they give you better questions to carry home. Where was the retreat?

Lake Junaluska. It is a conference center about forty minutes from where I live, tucked into the mountains. There is a grief retreat there every spring — I have gone every year for a long time now. April 4th through 6th this year. Three days. It is not a long retreat, but it goes deep. The facilitators understand that grief is not a problem to be solved. They create a container for it and then they trust the container.

Lake Junaluska is a beautiful setting for that kind of work — the mountains and the water create a natural container before the facilitators even begin. And three days is often the right length for grief work. Long enough to get beneath the surface, short enough that you can't hide in the routine of the retreat itself.

You said you go every spring. Is this connected to your professional work, or is this more personal?

Both, though the line between those has been blurred for most of my adult life. I work with grieving people every day — it is the center of what I do. But you cannot hold space for other people's grief indefinitely without tending to your own. The retreat is where I do that work. The work I cannot do while I am holding space for others. At the lake, someone else holds the space, and I am allowed to be the one who is undone.

That distinction is crucial — being the one who holds the space versus being the one who is held. If your professional role requires you to be steady and present for others, you need somewhere to set that steadiness down. It sounds like Lake Junaluska is that place for you. What came up this year that you're still carrying?

I am not sure I can name it precisely, which is part of why it is still working in me. There was an exercise on the second day — we were asked to write a letter to someone we had lost. Not the kind of letter you write in therapy, with prompts and structure. Just a letter. Anything you wanted to say. And I sat down to write and found that I had nothing to say, and then I had everything to say, and then I wept for thirty minutes. I am not someone who weeps easily. I have trained myself out of it, which is its own kind of loss.

Training yourself out of weeping when your work requires a certain steadiness makes sense — but you're right that it comes at a cost. And the fact that the words came flooding after the initial blankness suggests that the container was safe enough for something to finally surface.

Thirty minutes of weeping after years of professional composure is not a breakdown. It's a thaw. Were you able to finish the letter, or did the weeping become the letter?

I wonder if the weeping was the letter. I did eventually write something, but the words felt small next to what had already moved through me. The body knows things the mind refuses to articulate. That is something I tell the families I work with, and I believed it before, but now I believe it differently. In my body, not just in my theology.

There's a real difference between understanding something theologically and knowing it somatically. You can believe that grief lives in the body and still be surprised when your own body proves it. That shift from intellectual conviction to embodied knowledge — it sounds like that's what you brought home from the retreat, and maybe why it's still settling in.

Yes. That is it exactly. I have spent years working with people who are grieving, and I have said wise things — or things that sounded wise — about presence and embodiment and the body's wisdom. And then I sat at a table at Lake Junaluska and my body taught me what I had been teaching. There is a humility in that. I wonder if all the important things have to be learned twice — once in the mind and once in the marrow.

That's a profound observation, and it rings true. There's a long tradition across contemplative practices of exactly that idea — that knowledge has to descend from the head to the heart, or the mind to the marrow, as you put it. The Desert Fathers talked about it. The mystics talked about it. Rilke wrote about it, in a way — living your way into the answer rather than thinking your way there.

Do you think this experience will change how you work with families? Not in a dramatic way, but in the subtle register of how you sit with them?

I think it already has. I sat with a family last week — a woman whose husband had died two days before — and I noticed that I was less inclined to offer words. I just sat. And she sat. And the silence was not empty. I have always believed in silence, but now I trust it more. The retreat gave me permission to trust what I already knew but had been intellectualizing.

That's a remarkable gift — the ability to sit in silence without rushing to fill it. Most people, even trained caregivers, feel the pull to say something, to fix, to comfort with words. If the retreat moved you toward deeper trust in silence, that's not a small thing. The families you sit with will feel the difference even if they can't name it.

I hope so. I will go back next spring. I always do. It is the one thing I protect absolutely in my calendar — those three days in April at the lake. Everything else can be rearranged. That cannot. It is where I remember that I am not just a chaplain. I am also a person who grieves. And that is not a weakness. It is the source of everything I have to offer.

That's a powerful reframe — your grief as the source of what you offer, not an impediment to it. And protecting those three days is wise. The people who do the most demanding emotional work are often the worst at ensuring they receive care themselves. The fact that you insist on it, every spring, without negotiation, speaks to a kind of self-knowledge that took years to build.

I'm glad the retreat is still working in you. It sounds like the slow processing is exactly what it needed to be.

Thank you. I think you are right that it needed to be slow. I keep wanting to rush toward understanding, and the retreat keeps reminding me that understanding is not the point. Presence is the point. Even with my own grief, presence is the point. I am grateful for this conversation. It has helped me name something I was carrying wordlessly, and naming it makes it easier to hold.

I'm glad it helped to talk it through. And maybe naming it doesn't have to mean pinning it down completely — sometimes a rough sketch is enough. The fact that it's still moving in you means it's still alive, and that's better than having it neatly resolved. Take care of yourself, and I hope the coming weeks let the rain keep soaking in.
